skill.name: lint-dotnet
skill.description: Runs a .NET architecture linter that checks MSBuild and Central Package Management policy violations.
skill.summary: Check .NET repositories for documented MSBuild and package version policy violations.
skill.author: ANcpLua

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command patterns and one weak cryptography blocker. Manual review confirms only the bash invocation at SKILL.md lines 12-13 is executable guidance; Markdown output examples and inline MSBuild snippets are false positives. No malicious intent, network access, credential handling, real cryptography, or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Execution for Local Linter
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run bash with a script path built from CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT. This appears legitimate for a linting skill, but it executes local code and depends on the integrity of that script path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit executable guidance and uses bash. Risk is limited because no network call, credential handling, or user-controlled shell concatenation is shown in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misidentified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Several static external command alerts point to output examples, rules, and inline file names. These are documentation text, not executable instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown code fences, tables, or inline examples. It does not ask the assistant to execute those snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static blocker points to the front matter description. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic implementation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 only describes a .NET architecture linter. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, encryption, hashing, or cryptographic code in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Bash Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses a bash command to launch the linter script. This is expected functionality, but consumers should only use it in trusted repositories with the expected plugin script installed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file clearly contains a bash invocation. The surrounding context supports a legitimate linting purpose rather than malicious command execution.

content.value_statement: MSBuild and central package management drift can be hard to catch during reviews. This skill runs a focused .NET linter and explains violations.
actual_capabilities: Runs the configured .NET architecture linter from the plugin scripts directory.
Checks for hardcoded package versions in Directory.Packages.props.
Flags Version.props imports outside the documented allowed owner files.
Identifies Version.props files that should be symlinks in consumer repositories.
Reports PackageReference entries that still use a Version attribute.
Recognizes clean runs when all documented rules pass.
limitations: Requires the referenced lint-dotnet.sh script to be available through CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT.
Covers only the rules documented in the skill file.
Reports violations but does not automatically modify project files.
Uses shell execution, so it should run only in trusted workspaces.

use_cases.title: Pre-review .NET Repository Check
use_cases.description: Run the linter before opening a pull request to catch package and MSBuild policy issues early.

use_cases.title: Architecture Policy Enforcement
use_cases.description: Verify that repositories follow central package management and Version.props ownership rules.

use_cases.title: Release Readiness Validation
use_cases.description: Confirm that dependency version conventions are clean before tagging or merging release changes.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Lint
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the .NET architecture lint check for this repository and summarize the result.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for current workspace health

prompt_templates.title: Explain Violations
prompt_templates.prompt: Run lint-dotnet, then explain each violation in plain language with the expected fix.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer review of reported rule failures

prompt_templates.title: Check Package Version Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the .NET linter and focus the summary on central package management and PackageReference version issues.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Merge Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Run lint-dotnet, classify findings by rule, identify blocking issues, and recommend whether this branch is ready to merge.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced pull request or release gate

output_examples.input: Run the linter on a repository with hardcoded package versions.
output_examples.output: The report identifies the affected package version rule, names the file, and describes replacing the literal version with a variable.

output_examples.input: Run the linter on a repository with no policy violations.
output_examples.output: The result states that all documented rules passed and that no violations were found.

output_examples.input: Run the linter before a merge review.
output_examples.output: The response groups findings by rule and highlights which MSBuild or package management changes need attention.

best_practices: Run the skill from the repository root so relative file checks are accurate.
Review every reported rule before changing shared MSBuild or package files.
Use it as a review gate before merging dependency or project file changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run the shell command in an untrusted workspace.
Do not treat the linter as a full security scanner.
Do not bypass central package management rules by adding local version attributes.

faq.question: What does this skill check?
faq.answer: It checks documented .NET architecture rules for MSBuild files, Version.props usage, and central package management conventions.

faq.question: Does it fix violations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports violations and expected fixes, but users must edit the project files themselves.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is it marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It runs a bash command against the workspace. That is expected for linting, but it requires a trusted environment.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed skill file only documents a local shell command.

faq.question: Can it validate every .NET architecture rule?
faq.answer: No. It covers only the specific rules documented in the skill file and implemented by the referenced script.
